summary: Gruber draws a parallel between the early Mac era and the current iPhone platform, arguing that 'iPhone-likeness' is a clear, definable design philosophy in a way that 'Mac-likeness' no longer is. He proposes that iPhone UI design should minimize complexity and polish what remains, using Apple's built-in Notes app as a case study. The Notes app exemplifies iPhone-like design through its radical simplicity: just two screens, no preferences, automatic saving, and chronological sorting. Gruber argues many third-party developers fail to grasp iPhone-like design because they bring preconceptions from Mac software development, adding unnecessary complexity where restraint is needed.
key_points: The iPhone platform is at the stage the Mac was in the 1980s — new enough that 'iPhone-likeness' is a clear, meaningful design standard, unlike 'Mac-likeness' which has become vague hand-waving
Gruber's central iPhone UI guideline: figure out the absolute least you need to implement the idea, do just that, and polish the hell out of the experience
Apple's Notes app is a masterclass in iPhone-like design — two screens, no preferences, no explicit save, no folders, no sorting options
Many third-party iPhone developers fail because they can't shake preconceptions from designing Mac software, adding density and complexity where clarity and sparseness are needed
The Notes app's few shortcomings (no syncing, no search, no rotation) represent genuine missing features rather than design philosophy failures
money_quotes: Figure out the absolute least you need to do to implement the idea, do just that, and then polish the hell out of the experience.
In most cases, when someone complains 'that's not Mac-like', what they really mean is 'I don't like that.'
The iPhone, on the other hand, is very much where the Mac was in the 1980s.
Many third-party iPhone developers aren't wholly getting the iPhone-like part. In many cases, I think, it's because they can't shake preconceptions forged designing Mac software.
Clarity is more valuable than density.
The iPhone's Notes app is clear and sparse — or, perhaps better put, clear because it is sparse.
There no longer exists a single, simple, overall design style or philosophy that defines Mac-likeness.
Eschew preferences as much as possible, and assume that nearly all users will use the default settings.
key_insight: iPhone-like design means radical reduction — strip away every feature and UI element that isn't essential, then obsessively polish what remains.
